message:
feat(core): LabRuntime test infrastructure (wa-a4goc)

Build deterministic testing infrastructure using asupersync's LabRuntime:

- tests/common/lab.rs: run_lab_test, run_chaos_test, run_exploration_test,
  run_multi_seed_test helpers with LabTestConfig/ChaosTestConfig builders,
  structured tracing, and oracle assertion checking
- tests/common/fixtures.rs: MockPool (Semaphore+Mutex), MockMuxClient,
  MockUnixStream pairs, SimulatedNetwork with fault injection,
  RuntimeFixture, cancellation helpers (timeout_cx, healthy_cx)
- tests/lab_smoke.rs: 20 smoke tests exercising all helpers
- benches/labruntime_overhead.rs: 12 Criterion benchmarks covering
  setup/teardown, oracle check, virtual time, task spawn, DPOR
  exploration, and LabRuntime vs tokio comparison

All 20 tests pass. All 12 benchmark groups compile and run.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-1ta8j","title":"Proactive pane lifecycle management — zombie reaping, stuck detection, age-based cleanup","description":"# Proactive Pane Lifecycle Management

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization, /idea-wizard

## Problem
At 200+ panes, abandoned/stuck agents accumulate silently. A 54-hour agent consuming 118GB RAM was observed in production. Zombie processes consume process table slots. Stuck test processes (12+ hours) waste CPU.

## Solution: Activity-Based Lifecycle Engine

**NOTE**: This bead CONSUMES process_triage (wa-2zywl) as a library — it does NOT reimplement zombie reaping or stuck detection. process_triage provides the ProcessClassifier/TriageEngine/WhackAMoleDetector; this bead adds the FrankenTerm-specific health classification and pane-aware policies on top.

### Pane Health Classification (from /system-performance-remediation)
| Age | CPU% | Status | Action |
|-----|------|--------|--------|
| <4h | >10% | Active | Protect |
| <4h | <2% | Thinking | Protect |
| 4-16h | >5% | Working | Check children |
| 4-16h | <2% | Possibly stuck | Flag for review |
| 16-24h | any | Likely stuck | Kill if only MCP children |
| >24h | any | Abandoned | Kill immediately |

### Components to build

1. **PaneHealthMonitor**: Background task sampling per-pane CPU%, memory, child count, age
   - Sample every 30s (configurable)
   - Store in ring buffer for trend analysis
   - Expose via MCP tool and CLI
   - **macOS**: Use libproc/proc_pidinfo instead of /proc/[pid]/stat

2. **PaneTriagePolicy**: Wraps process_triage TriageEngine with pane-aware context
   - Maps pane health classification to process_triage kill hierarchy
   - Adds FrankenTerm-specific categories: "agent thinking" vs "agent stuck"
   - Per-pane policy overrides via configuration

3. **AgeReaper**: Configurable age-based cleanup policies
   - Default: warn at 16h, kill at 24h
   - Per-pane overrides via configuration
   - Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM, wait 30s, SIGKILL

4. **Resource pressure response**: When PSI avg10 > 30% (Linux) or CPU load > 80% (macOS)
   - Renice oldest idle panes to nice=19, ionice class 3 (idle)
   - Pause non-essential captures
   - Warn user via notification

## Formal requirement
The stuck detector whack-a-mole logic lives in process_triage (wa-2zywl) with formal verification. This bead only needs to verify that PaneTriagePolicy correctly maps pane context to process_triage categories.

## Tests
- Simulate 200 panes with various ages/CPU profiles, verify correct classification
- Test PaneTriagePolicy maps to process_triage categories correctly
- Test age reaper respects graceful shutdown
- Load test: 200 pane health checks in <100ms total
- **Use criterion benchmarks for health check latency**

## Acceptance criteria
- Health classification matches the table above
- Delegates zombie/stuck/whack-a-mole to process_triage library
- Age reaper respects graceful shutdown
- CPU pressure triggers renice within 5s
- Works on both Linux (/proc) and macOS (libproc)
- All under /extreme-software-optimization methodology: profile before/after","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:10:51.270912Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:08:24.676026-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T00:08:24.676026-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1ta8j","depends_on_id":"wa-2zywl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:14:17.414441Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1ta8j","depends_on_id":"wa-a0owq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:14:17.553827Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1u55z","title":"Replace TCP networking and TLS (tokio-rustls → asupersync)","description":"# Replace TCP networking and TLS

## Background
TCP and TLS are used by feature-gated modules: web server (web.rs), distributed mode (distributed.rs), and HTTP clients (reqwest).

## Scope

### TCP (web.rs)
- TcpListener for the HTTP API server
- Bound to configurable port, serves /health, /panes, /events, /search, etc.
- Currently uses fastapi::TcpServer wrapper around tokio TcpListener

### TLS (distributed.rs)
- tokio-rustls 0.26 for mTLS between distributed nodes
- Certificate and private key loading
- TLS 1.2+ configuration
- Client and server TLS config

### HTTP Client (reqwest)
- reqwest 0.12.12 with rustls-tls feature
- Used for outbound API calls
- Must be replaced with asupersync::http client

## Migration
```rust
// TCP Before
use tokio::net::TcpListener;
let listener = TcpListener::bind(addr).await?;

// TCP After
use asupersync::net::TcpListener;
let listener = TcpListener::bind(cx, addr).await?;

// TLS Before
use tokio_rustls::TlsConnector;

// TLS After
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnector;  // rustls-based, no tokio dep

// HTTP Before
let resp = reqwest::get(url).await?;

// HTTP After
let client = asupersync::http::Client::new();
let resp = client.get(cx, url).await?;
```

## Note on FastAPI/FastMCP
These vendored frameworks are built on tokio. They will need either:
1. Porting to asupersync (significant effort)
2. Replacing with asupersync-native HTTP/MCP server (if available)
3. Running in a compatibility shim (last resort)

This bead covers the direct TCP/TLS replacement. FastAPI/FastMCP refactoring is tracked separately.

## Acceptance criteria
- TCP server binds and accepts connections via asupersync
- TLS handshake works with asupersync TLS
- HTTP client makes successful outbound requests
- reqwest dependency removed
- tokio-rustls dependency removed

## macOS platform note
- **kqueue for TCP readiness**: On macOS, asupersync uses kqueue (not epoll) for TCP socket readiness notification (accept, read, write events). Verify that asupersync's reactor correctly uses kqueue for TcpListener and TcpStream on Darwin targets. If any platform-specific code exists for TCP handling, ensure `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]` paths use kqueue and `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` paths use epoll. This is especially important for the web server's accept loop and the distributed module's TLS connections.

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for TLS handshake and throughput**: Add `benches/tcp_tls.rs` measuring:
  - TCP connection establishment latency (bind + connect + accept)
  - TLS handshake latency (client hello → finished) for TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3
  - mTLS handshake latency (mutual certificate verification)
  - Sustained TCP throughput (MB/s) for streaming data over plain TCP
  - Sustained TLS throughput (MB/s) for streaming data over TLS
  - Comparison with tokio-rustls for equivalent TLS operations

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Concurrent connection testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to test concurrent TCP/TLS scenarios:
  - Multiple clients connecting to TcpListener simultaneously — verify all connections accepted, no drops
  - Interleaved TLS handshakes — verify no state corruption between concurrent handshakes
  - Race between connection accept and listener shutdown — verify clean teardown
  - Concurrent reads and writes on established TLS connections — verify no data corruption or reordering","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:49:05.178507Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:53:39.465924Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1u55z","depends_on_id":"wa-2abzy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:21.834643Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1u55z","depends_on_id":"wa-1yz79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:24.65586Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

## Success Criteria

- Overrides are visible via status/health output","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:05:00.210578659Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:36:32.039776-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:36:32.03978-05:00"}

 {"id":"wa-1uqi","title":"FTUI-03.2.a Route in-process logs through single output sink","description":"## Background
In-process output must obey one-writer constraints to keep rendering deterministic and avoid output corruption.

## Deliverables
- inventory and removal plan for direct stdout/stderr writes in UI-active paths
- unified output sink routing contract for logs/events/status lines
- verification checks to prove no bypass writes remain

## Acceptance Criteria
- all targeted paths route through the sanctioned sink while UI is active
- checks detect and fail on new bypass writes
- validation evidence includes unit assertions and runtime logs demonstrating routing correctness.,","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmLynx","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:05.996650629Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T05:12:15.499308732Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T05:12:15.49917289Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1uqi","depends_on_id":"wa-3cso","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:06.014840191Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1uqi","depends_on_id":"wa-3g47","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:35.653843104Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1utb","title":"FTUI-02.1 Add ftui dependency stack and feature-gated integration path","description":"## Background\nwa must consume /dp/frankentui intentionally, not ad-hoc.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Cargo feature strategy for ftui adoption path\n- path/git pin policy to /dp/frankentui\n- explicit toggles for legacy/new tui runtime\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- project builds with and without ftui migration feature\n- dependency graph is documented and deterministic.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:39.031226995Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:18:40.996712962Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:18:40.996571069Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1utb","depends_on_id":"wa-1k52","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:39.046828394Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1utb","depends_on_id":"wa-136q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:15:45.984694603Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963666064Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963516365Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-2ahu0","title":"Memory pressure engine — per-pane budgets, eviction, and compression","description":"# Memory Pressure Engine

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization, /alien-artifact-coding

## Problem
200 agents × 500MB = 100GB baseline. Long-lived agents grow to 1-10GB+ each. Without pressure management, OOM killer strikes randomly, potentially killing the mux server itself.

## Solution

### 1. Per-pane memory tracking
- **Linux**: Sample /proc/[pid]/statm every 30s for each pane process tree
- **macOS**: Use proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDTASKINFO) for per-process RSS, virtual, shared memory
- Track RSS, virtual, shared memory per pane
- Compute total memory pressure: sum(pane RSS) / available RAM

### 2. Memory pressure tiers
- **Linux**: Keyed to /proc/pressure/memory PSI metrics
- **macOS**: Keyed to vm_statistics64 (free + inactive pages ratio) and os_proc_available_memory()
- avg10 < 10% (or macOS equivalent): Green — no action
- avg10 10-20%: Yellow — compress idle pane scrollback, warn user
- avg10 20-40%: Orange — evict oldest idle pane scrollback to disk, pause captures
- avg10 > 40%: Red — kill largest idle pane, emergency scrollback eviction

### 3. Scrollback compression and eviction
- Compress scrollback for panes idle > 5 min (zstd, ~10:1 ratio)
- Evict scrollback to disk for panes idle > 30 min
- Restore transparently on pane focus

### 4. OOM score adjustment
- **Linux**: Set oom_score_adj to protect mux server (-1000)
- **macOS**: No OOM score equivalent; use Jetsam priority via memorystatus_control (if available) or advisory
- Set agent panes proportional to age: newer = lower score, older = higher score
- Ensures OOM killer targets oldest idle agents first

### 5. Formal memory bound (/alien-artifact-coding)
Prove that the mux server own memory usage is bounded by O(n) where n = pane count, independent of scrollback size. Use formal methods.

## Tests
- Simulate memory pressure ramp, verify tier transitions
- Measure scrollback compression ratio and latency
- Verify OOM score adj protects mux server (Linux)
- 200-pane memory stress test: no OOM with 128GB limit
- **Use criterion benchmarks for compression throughput**
- **Test both Linux and macOS code paths**

## Acceptance criteria
- Memory pressure monitored via PSI (Linux) / vm_statistics64 (macOS)
- Scrollback compression saves >80% memory for idle panes
- Disk eviction transparent to user
- Mux server never killed by OOM
- Memory bound formally verified","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:11:54.455402Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T23:39:25.878472-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T23:39:25.878472-05:00","close_reason":"Memory pressure engine implemented: MemoryPressureTier, MemoryAction, MemoryPressureMonitor with Linux /proc/meminfo and macOS vm_stat/sysctl. Per-pane memory tracking types. 21 tests passing."}
 {"id":"wa-2apg5","title":"Zellij session management analysis — persistence, reconnect, resurrection","description":"# Zellij Session Management Analysis

## Why this matters
Zellij has built-in session persistence and resurrection — a user can detach and reattach, and sessions survive server restarts. FrankenTerm's session persistence epic (wa-rsaf) is building this from scratch. Understanding how Zellij solved it could save significant design effort.

## What to analyze

### Session lifecycle
- How are sessions created, listed, attached, detached, destroyed?
- What state is persisted and where (filesystem, database, memory)?
- What happens on unexpected server crash vs clean shutdown?

### Session resurrection
- Can Zellij restore sessions after a server restart?
- What state is lost vs preserved (scrollback? running processes? environment?)
- How does it handle process re-launch (or does it?)

### Multi-client attachment
- Can multiple clients attach to the same session simultaneously?
- How are conflicts handled (input routing, resize, permissions)?
- This is directly relevant to FrankenTerm's agent swarm use case

### Session serialization format
- What format does Zellij use to serialize session state?
- How does it handle layout topology (splits, tabs, floating panes)?
- Compare to our planned MuxSnapshot schema (bd-cuz)

## FrankenTerm implications
For each finding, document: "FrankenTerm could use this by..." with a concrete implementation sketch.

## Acceptance criteria
- Session lifecycle fully documented
- Resurrection mechanism analyzed (what survives, what doesn't)
- Multi-client behavior analyzed
- Serialization format documented
- 3+ actionable recommendations for FrankenTerm

## Cross-references
- **wa-rsaf** (session persistence epic): Findings from this analysis directly inform the design of FrankenTerm's session persistence system. Specific areas of impact include: session serialization format (compare with MuxSnapshot schema bd-cuz), resurrection strategy (what state to persist and how), and multi-client attachment semantics for agent swarms.

## Notes
- This is one of the highest-value Zellij analysis beads because FrankenTerm's session persistence (wa-rsaf) is being designed from scratch. Zellij's battle-tested approach to session resurrection can save significant design iteration.
- Pay special attention to what state Zellij does NOT persist — these are likely the hard problems where persistence has diminishing returns.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"RusticSnow","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:53:42.044778Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:33:53.114172-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:33:53.114172-05:00","close_reason":"Delivered evidence/zellij/session-management.md","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2apg5","depends_on_id":"wa-okyhm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:56.544663Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2apg5","depends_on_id":"wa-22o1q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:01.973841Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

-{"id":"wa-2bai5","title":"Zellij analysis synthesis — improvement roadmap for FrankenTerm mux server","description":"# Zellij Analysis Synthesis

## Goal
Synthesize ALL Zellij analysis findings into a prioritized, actionable improvement roadmap for FrankenTerm mux server.

## Deliverable: Comparison report with three sections

### 1. Quick wins (low effort, high value)
Ideas we can adopt immediately with minimal refactoring. Each with:
- What Zellij does
- How FrankenTerm would implement it
- Estimated effort (hours/days)
- Which existing beads it relates to

### 2. Strategic improvements (medium effort, high value)
Architectural ideas worth investing in. Each with:
- Design sketch
- Migration path from current architecture
- Risk assessment
- New beads to create

### 3. Future considerations (high effort or uncertain value)
Ideas worth tracking but not immediately actionable. Each with:
- Why it interesting
- What would need to change in FrankenTerm
- Conditions under which it becomes worth doing

## Cross-reference (MUST link findings to these epics)
- wa-rsaf (session persistence) — session management findings
- wa-3kxe (fork hardening) — architecture hardening findings
- wa-3cyp (performance optimization) — throughput/scaling findings
- wa-e34d9 (asupersync migration) — async runtime findings
- wa-iehgn (ultra-performance) — resource management, scheduling findings
- wa-dr6zv (/dp tool integration) — plugin/extensibility findings
- bd-9dp (tiered update rates) — output throttling findings
- wa-3r5e (scrollback memory) — memory management findings
- **wa-3bja.5** (Ghostty analysis synthesis): Both synthesis reports should be compared side-by-side to produce a unified improvement roadmap. Where Zellij and Ghostty solve the same problem differently, document both approaches and recommend the best fit for FrankenTerm.

## Unified Roadmap Note
After both wa-2bai5 (this bead) and wa-3bja.5 (Ghostty synthesis) are complete, a comparison pass should identify:
- Patterns where both Zellij and Ghostty converge (high-confidence recommendations)
- Patterns where they diverge (requires FrankenTerm-specific analysis)
- Unique innovations from each that have no counterpart in the other

## Acceptance criteria
- All 6 analysis beads synthesized
- Prioritized roadmap with 5+ actionable recommendations
- Each recommendation has implementation sketch and effort estimate
- Cross-references to ALL relevant existing beads (not just wa-rsaf/wa-3kxe/wa-3cyp/wa-e34d9)
- New beads created for any recommendations that do not map to existing work
- Explicit comparison notes prepared for cross-referencing with wa-3bja.5","notes":"Claimed by EmeraldHawk. +{"id":"wa-2bai5","title":"Zellij analysis synthesis — improvement roadmap for FrankenTerm mux server","description":"# Zellij Analysis Synthesis

## Goal
Synthesize ALL Zellij analysis findings into a prioritized, actionable improvement roadmap for FrankenTerm mux server.

## Deliverable: Comparison report with three sections

### 1. Quick wins (low effort, high value)
Ideas we can adopt immediately with minimal refactoring. Each with:
- What Zellij does
- How FrankenTerm would implement it
- Estimated effort (hours/days)
- Which existing beads it relates to

### 2. Strategic improvements (medium effort, high value)
Architectural ideas worth investing in. Each with:
- Design sketch
- Migration path from current architecture
- Risk assessment
- New beads to create

### 3. Future considerations (high effort or uncertain value)
Ideas worth tracking but not immediately actionable. Each with:
- Why it interesting
- What would need to change in FrankenTerm
- Conditions under which it becomes worth doing

## Cross-reference (MUST link findings to these epics)
- wa-rsaf (session persistence) — session management findings
- wa-3kxe (fork hardening) — architecture hardening findings
- wa-3cyp (performance optimization) — throughput/scaling findings
- wa-e34d9 (asupersync migration) — async runtime findings
- wa-iehgn (ultra-performance) — resource management, scheduling findings
- wa-dr6zv (/dp tool integration) — plugin/extensibility findings
- bd-9dp (tiered update rates) — output throttling findings
- wa-3r5e (scrollback memory) — memory management findings
- **wa-3bja.5** (Ghostty analysis synthesis): Both synthesis reports should be compared side-by-side to produce a unified improvement roadmap. Where Zellij and Ghostty solve the same problem differently, document both approaches and recommend the best fit for FrankenTerm.

## Unified Roadmap Note
After both wa-2bai5 (this bead) and wa-3bja.5 (Ghostty synthesis) are complete, a comparison pass should identify:
- Patterns where both Zellij and Ghostty converge (high-confidence recommendations)
- Patterns where they diverge (requires FrankenTerm-specific analysis)
- Unique innovations from each that have no counterpart in the other

## Acceptance criteria
- All 6 analysis beads synthesized
- Prioritized roadmap with 5+ actionable recommendations
- Each recommendation has implementation sketch and effort estimate
- Cross-references to ALL relevant existing beads (not just wa-rsaf/wa-3kxe/wa-3cyp/wa-e34d9)
- New beads created for any recommendations that do not map to existing work
- Explicit comparison notes prepared for cross-referencing with wa-3bja.5","notes":"Completed synthesis artifact: docs/zellij-analysis-synthesis.md. Synthesized all required Zellij analysis beads (wa-okyhm, wa-vcjbi, wa-2apg5, wa-1pygr, wa-1xk9j, wa-1pgzt), produced prioritized quick wins/strategic/future roadmap with effort+implementation sketches, added explicit Ghostty side-by-side convergence/divergence notes for wa-3bja.5, and created follow-on beads wa-1u9qw, wa-33uf8, wa-3jewu for uncovered work.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"EmeraldHawk","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:50.149265Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:45:29.232733Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:45:29.227703Z","close_reason":"Completed synthesis report and created follow-on beads","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2bai5","depends_on_id":"wa-okyhm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:57.048971Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2bai5","depends_on_id":"wa-2apg5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:08.435433Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2bai5","depends_on_id":"wa-1pygr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:08.540599Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2bai5","depends_on_id":"wa-1xk9j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:08.6407Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2bai5","depends_on_id":"wa-vcjbi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:08.738271Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2bai5","depends_on_id":"wa-1pgzt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:08.839331Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2c50","title":"Protocol error auto-recovery — reconnect on UnexpectedResponse","description":"## Goal
Implement automatic recovery from WezTerm mux protocol errors (UnexpectedResponse, codec failures) by detecting corrupted connection state, poisoning the connection, and transparently reconnecting. Build on the existing circuit_breaker.rs and retry.rs modules.

## Background & Motivation
Under heavy load, the WezTerm mux protocol can enter a corrupted state where the client and server are "out of sync" on the PDU stream. This manifests as UnexpectedResponse errors (e.g., getting UnitResponse when expecting ListPanesResponse). The error is defined at mux_client.rs line 83.

Currently, wa's RetryWith mechanism retries with the SAME connection, which has corrupted state. This makes the retry fail too, creating a cascade of errors. The fix is to detect protocol corruption, discard the bad connection, and create a fresh one.

## Existing Code (BUILD ON, don't reinvent)
- **circuit_breaker.rs**: CircuitBreaker with Open/HalfOpen/Closed states, configurable failure threshold and recovery timeout. Already handles "stop trying after too many failures" pattern.
- **retry.rs**: RetryPolicy with configurable max_retries, backoff strategy (constant, linear, exponential), jitter. Already handles "try again with backoff" pattern.
- **pool.rs**: Pool<C> with health_check(), idle eviction, semaphore concurrency limiting.
- **degradation.rs**: DegradationManager with Subsystem::WeztermCli for reporting mux failures.

## Technical Design

### Error Classification
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs

pub enum ProtocolErrorKind {
    Recoverable,   // UnexpectedResponse, timeout — reconnect and retry
    Permanent,     // Version mismatch, auth failure — give up
    Transient,     // Server busy, try again later
}

impl DirectMuxClient {
    fn classify_error(&self, err: &Error) -> ProtocolErrorKind {
        match err {
            Error::UnexpectedResponse { .. } => ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable,
            Error::IoError(e) if e.kind() == ErrorKind::BrokenPipe => ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable,
            Error::IoError(e) if e.kind() == ErrorKind::ConnectionReset => ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable,
            Error::CodecVersionMismatch => ProtocolErrorKind::Permanent,
            _ => ProtocolErrorKind::Transient,
        }
    }
}
```

### Auto-Recovery with Circuit Breaker Integration
```rust
// Wire error classification into existing CircuitBreaker + RetryPolicy:
pub struct ResilientMuxPool {
    pool: DirectMuxClientPool,
    circuit_breaker: CircuitBreaker,  // from circuit_breaker.rs
    retry_policy: RetryPolicy,        // from retry.rs
    degradation: Arc<DegradationManager>,
}

impl ResilientMuxPool {
    pub async fn execute<F, T>(&self, op: F) -> Result<T>
    where
        F: Fn(&DirectMuxClient) -> Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = Result<T>>>>,
    {
        // Check circuit breaker first
        if !self.circuit_breaker.allow_request() {
            return Err(Error::CircuitOpen);
        }

        let result = self.retry_policy.execute(|| async {
            let conn = self.pool.get().await?;
            match op(&conn).await {
                Ok(result) => {
                    self.circuit_breaker.record_success();
                    Ok(result)
                }
                Err(e) => {
                    let kind = conn.classify_error(&e);
                    match kind {
                        ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable => {
                            conn.mark_poisoned(); // pool will create fresh
                            self.circuit_breaker.record_failure();
                            Err(e) // retry with fresh connection
                        }
                        ProtocolErrorKind::Permanent => {
                            self.circuit_breaker.record_failure();
                            self.degradation.report_failure(Subsystem::WeztermCli, &e.to_string()).await;
                            Err(Error::Permanent(e)) // don't retry
                        }
                        ProtocolErrorKind::Transient => {
                            Err(e) // retry after backoff
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }).await;

        result
    }
}
```

### Frame Resynchronization
For cases where the PDU stream is corrupted mid-frame:
```rust
/// If read_next_pdu() fails to decode, skip bytes until a valid PDU header is found
async fn resync_stream(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
    let mut buf = [0u8; 1];
    let mut skipped = 0;
    loop {
        self.stream.read_exact(&mut buf).await?;
        skipped += 1;
        if self.try_parse_pdu_header().is_ok() {
            warn!("Resynchronized PDU stream after skipping {} bytes", skipped);
            return Ok(());
        }
        if skipped > 1_000_000 {
            return Err(Error::StreamCorrupted);
        }
    }
}
```

## Existing Code References
- **UnexpectedResponse**: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs line 83
- **CircuitBreaker**: crates/wa-core/src/circuit_breaker.rs — Open/HalfOpen/Closed FSM
- **RetryPolicy**: crates/wa-core/src/retry.rs — exponential backoff with jitter
- **Pool<C>**: crates/wa-core/src/pool.rs — has mark_poisoned() / is_healthy() pattern
- **DegradationManager**: crates/wa-core/src/degradation.rs — Subsystem::WeztermCli

## Expected Impact
- Protocol errors no longer cascade into sustained failures
- Transparent to callers — just a brief retry delay
- Circuit breaker prevents hammering an unhealthy mux server
- Combined with connection pool, makes wa resilient to mux server instability

## Dependencies
- bd-41w (Connection pool): Required for transparent reconnection

## Acceptance Criteria
- UnexpectedResponse triggers connection poisoning and retry
- Retry with fresh connection succeeds
- Frame resynchronization recovers from partial PDU reads
- Permanent errors (version mismatch) are NOT retried
- Circuit breaker activates after configurable failure threshold
- Integration with existing CircuitBreaker and RetryPolicy modules
- Metrics: recovery count, resync count, permanent failures, circuit breaker state

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation, 1 hour testing

## Concurrency Testing (LabRuntime DPOR)
- **LabRuntime with DPOR exploration** for concurrent reconnection scenarios:
  - Multiple tasks simultaneously discover a poisoned connection and attempt reconnection. DPOR explores all interleavings to verify that exactly one new connection is established (no thundering herd), the circuit breaker state transitions are linearizable, and no task observes a half-initialized connection.
  - Concurrent error classification: multiple tasks classify errors on the same connection simultaneously. DPOR verifies that classification is deterministic regardless of interleaving and that mark_poisoned() is idempotent under concurrent calls.
  - Circuit breaker state machine under concurrent load: DPOR explores interleavings where record_failure() and record_success() race, verifying the FSM never enters an invalid state (e.g., Open with zero failures).

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/protocol_recovery.rs`:
  - `reconnect_latency`: measure time from error detection to successful retry on a fresh connection. Target: <10ms for local Unix socket reconnection.
  - `error_classification_throughput`: measure classify_error() throughput. Target: >1M classifications/sec (it should be a simple match).
  - `circuit_breaker_state_transition`: measure record_failure/record_success cycle latency. Target: <100ns per state update.

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Error classification correctness**: for any Error variant, classify_error() returns a deterministic ProtocolErrorKind. Specifically: all UnexpectedResponse errors map to Recoverable, all CodecVersionMismatch errors map to Permanent, BrokenPipe and ConnectionReset map to Recoverable.
- **Circuit breaker consistency**: for any sequence of record_failure() and record_success() calls, the circuit breaker state is always valid (Closed with failures < threshold, Open after threshold crossed, HalfOpen after recovery timeout).
- **Retry idempotency**: retrying a Recoverable error with a fresh connection produces the same result as a first attempt on a healthy connection (no state leakage between attempts).

## Cross-References
- **wa-1l2o** (Error cascade circuit breaker): provides the broader error cascade prevention framework that this bead's per-connection circuit breaker integrates with; wa-1l2o manages cross-subsystem cascades while wa-2c50 handles mux-protocol-specific recovery.","notes":"Landed mux pool protocol-error recovery with reconnect+retry + unit test; pushed to origin/main.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"RusticFinch","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:50.483477Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:34:41.773087-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:34:41.773087-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented protocol_recovery.rs with ProtocolErrorKind classification, RecoveryEngine (circuit breaker + retry), FrameCorruptionDetector, ConnectionHealthTracker. 40 tests passing.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2c50","depends_on_id":"wa-a27t","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:20.765571Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-2c50","depends_on_id":"wa-41w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:20.765571Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-2c50","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:36.165421Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2cha","title":"Mux server watchdog — health monitoring and auto-restart","description":"# Mux Server Watchdog — Health Monitoring and Auto-Restart

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization

## Goal
Extend the existing watchdog module (watchdog.rs, 489 lines) with a MuxWatchdog component that monitors WezTerm mux server health (responsiveness, memory usage, connection acceptance) and can trigger alerts, degradation, or controlled restart via the safe-restart workflow (wa-1igc).

## Background
The mux server can become unresponsive without actually crashing — alive (process exists, port open) but not serving requests (lock contention, memory pressure, stuck event loop). Observed on css: 76GB RSS after 23 days, connections timing out.

## Existing Code (EXTEND, do not create from scratch)
- **watchdog.rs** (489 lines): HeartbeatRegistry with per-subsystem atomic timestamps, health_check() with configurable stall thresholds
- **degradation.rs** (989 lines): DegradationManager with Subsystem::WeztermCli already exists
- **backpressure.rs** (657 lines): BackpressureManager with 4-tier FSM

## MuxWatchdog Health Check
Every 30s:
1. **Ping**: list panes with timeout (5s default)
2. **Memory check**:
   - **Linux**: Read /proc/<pid>/status VmRSS field
   - **macOS**: Use proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDTASKINFO) for resident_size, or `ps -o rss= -p <pid>`
3. Track health history in ring buffer (last 1000 samples)

## Escalation Logic
- 3 consecutive failures → DegradationManager alert (Subsystem::WeztermCli)
- 10 consecutive failures + auto_restart enabled → delegate to safe-restart workflow (wa-1igc)
- Memory > 32GB → warning
- Memory > 64GB → critical, report to DegradationManager

## Integration Points
- HeartbeatRegistry (watchdog.rs): Add mux_health atomic timestamp
- DegradationManager (degradation.rs): Use existing Subsystem::WeztermCli
- BackpressureMonitor: Feed health status into backpressure decisions
- Safe-restart (wa-1igc): Auto-restart delegates to snapshot→stop→start→restore

## Tests
- Health check detects unresponsive mux within 3 cycles
- Memory threshold alerts logged correctly
- **Criterion benchmarks**: health check overhead (<1ms per check)
- Test escalation: consecutive failures → degradation → restart trigger
- Test graceful handling of mux server not running at all
- **Test both Linux and macOS memory reading paths**

## Acceptance criteria
- Health checks run at configured interval
- Unresponsive mux detected within 3 check cycles
- Memory alerts reported to DegradationManager
- Auto-restart delegates to safe-restart workflow
- Works on Linux (/proc) and macOS (proc_pidinfo/ps)
- Health history available via wa robot API","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:36:17.627014Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:53:06.533797-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:53:06.533797-05:00","close_reason":"Mux server watchdog complete: MuxWatchdog in watchdog.rs with ping/memory checks, mux_watchdog.rs standalone module, degradation integration, wired into watcher","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2cha","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:39.678193Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2ck4","title":"Core: explain-match trace generation in pattern engine (bounded + redacted)","description":"# Task: Core explain-match trace generation

## Goal
Implement the **shared core** for explain-match traces inside the pattern engine.

This task is intentionally scoped to the *engine-level* trace primitive so multiple surfaces can reuse it:
- robot `rules test` output (owned by `wa-nu4.2.1.4`)
- human `wa rules test` output (owned by `wa-nu4.3.2.6`)
- incident bundles / diagnostics (bd-upg.1 + bd-upg.14.4)

## Requirements
- Provide a structured trace object for a match, including:
  - stable identifiers: `pack_id`, `rule_id`, (optional) `extractor_id`
  - evidence pointers (byte spans / named extracts) with truncation indicators
  - gating decisions: why a rule was eligible/ineligible (state gates)
  - boundedness metadata: what was truncated, counts, max bytes
- Redaction:
  - extracted fields and excerpts must pass through redaction
  - ensure "trace" cannot become a side-channel for secrets
- Deterministic ordering:
  - stable ordering of matches and fields
  - stable ordering within trace phases
- Performance:
  - tracing must be cheap when disabled
  - trace generation should be optionally toggled so hot-path detection isn't penalized

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - golden fixtures for trace structure
  - boundedness enforcement (explicit truncation markers)
  - redaction invariants (known secret patterns do not appear in trace output)
- Regression tests:
  - schema validation for the trace object (even if embedded in other command outputs)

## Acceptance Criteria
- A reusable explain-match trace primitive exists in the pattern engine.
- Traces are stable, bounded, redacted, and deterministic.
- Downstream surfaces can embed this trace without duplicating logic.

@@ -0,0 +1,579 @@
+//! LabRuntime test helpers for deterministic async testing.
+//!
+//! Provides ergonomic wrappers around asupersync's `LabRuntime` for:
+//! - Deterministic seed-based testing with `run_lab_test`
+//! - Chaos fault injection with `run_chaos_test`
+//! - Schedule exploration (DPOR) with `run_exploration_test`
+//!
+//! All helpers emit structured tracing logs with seed, test name, and
+//! outcome information for debugging.
+
+use asupersync::lab::chaos::ChaosConfig;
+use asupersync::lab::explorer::{ExplorerConfig, ExplorationReport, ScheduleExplorer};
+use asupersync::{Budget, LabConfig, LabRuntime, Time};
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// LabTestConfig — ergonomic builder for test-level config
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Configuration for a single lab test run.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
+pub struct LabTestConfig {
+    /// Deterministic seed for reproducibility.
+    pub seed: u64,
+    /// Human-readable test identifier (used in tracing output).
+    pub test_name: String,
+    /// Number of virtual workers to simulate.
+    pub worker_count: usize,
+    /// Maximum steps before the runtime forcibly terminates.
+    pub max_steps: u64,
+    /// Whether to panic when obligation leaks are detected.
+    pub panic_on_leak: bool,
+}
+
+impl LabTestConfig {
+    /// Create a config with the given seed and test name.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn new(seed: u64, test_name: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            seed,
+            test_name: test_name.into(),
+            worker_count: 2,
+            max_steps: 100_000,
+            panic_on_leak: true,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Set the number of virtual workers.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn worker_count(mut self, count: usize) -> Self {
+        self.worker_count = count;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Set the maximum number of steps.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn max_steps(mut self, steps: u64) -> Self {
+        self.max_steps = steps;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Whether to panic on obligation leaks (default: true).
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn panic_on_leak(mut self, value: bool) -> Self {
+        self.panic_on_leak = value;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Convert to an asupersync `LabConfig`.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn to_lab_config(&self) -> LabConfig {
+        LabConfig::new(self.seed)
+            .worker_count(self.worker_count)
+            .max_steps(self.max_steps)
+            .panic_on_leak(self.panic_on_leak)
+    }
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// LabTestReport — structured output from a lab test run
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Report from a single lab test execution.
+#[derive(Debug)]
+pub struct LabTestReport {
+    /// Seed used for this run.
+    pub seed: u64,
+    /// Test name.
+    pub test_name: String,
+    /// Number of steps executed.
+    pub steps: u64,
+    /// Final virtual time.
+    pub final_time: Time,
+    /// Whether all oracles passed.
+    pub oracles_passed: bool,
+    /// Whether any invariant violations were found.
+    pub invariant_violations_found: bool,
+}
+
+impl LabTestReport {
+    /// Returns true if the test passed all checks.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn passed(&self) -> bool {
+        self.oracles_passed && !self.invariant_violations_found
+    }
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// run_lab_test — deterministic test with oracle assertions
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Run a deterministic lab test with the given configuration.
+///
+/// The `test_fn` receives a mutable reference to a freshly constructed
+/// `LabRuntime`. It should set up tasks, drive execution (e.g. via
+/// `runtime.run_until_quiescent()`), and perform assertions.
+///
+/// After `test_fn` returns, this helper automatically:
+/// 1. Generates a structured `LabRunReport` with oracle results
+/// 2. Asserts all oracles pass and no invariant violations exist
+/// 3. Logs the outcome with structured tracing
+///
+/// # Panics
+///
+/// Panics if any oracle fails or invariant violations are detected.
+///
+/// # Example
+///
+/// ```ignore
+/// use crate::common::lab::{LabTestConfig, run_lab_test};
+///
+/// run_lab_test(
+///     LabTestConfig::new(42, "my_deterministic_test"),
+///     |runtime| {
+///         // Set up tasks in runtime...
+///         runtime.run_until_quiescent();
+///     },
+/// );
+/// ```
+pub fn run_lab_test<F>(config: LabTestConfig, test_fn: F) -> LabTestReport
+where
+    F: FnOnce(&mut LabRuntime),
+{
+    let seed = config.seed;
+    let test_name = config.test_name.clone();
+
+    tracing::info!(
+        seed,
+        test_name = %test_name,
+        workers = config.worker_count,
+        max_steps = config.max_steps,
+        "Starting LabRuntime test"
+    );
+
+    let lab_config = config.to_lab_config();
+    let mut runtime = LabRuntime::new(lab_config);
+
+    // Run the test closure
+    test_fn(&mut runtime);
+
+    // Collect oracle report
+    let report = runtime.run_until_quiescent_with_report();
+    let oracles_passed = report.oracle_report.all_passed();
+    let invariant_violations_found = !report.invariant_violations.is_empty();
+
+    let lab_report = LabTestReport {
+        seed,
+        test_name: test_name.clone(),
+        steps: runtime.steps(),
+        final_time: runtime.now(),
+        oracles_passed,
+        invariant_violations_found,
+    };
+
+    tracing::info!(
+        seed,
+        test_name = %test_name,
+        steps = runtime.steps(),
+        oracles_passed,
+        violations = report.invariant_violations.len(),
+        "LabRuntime test completed"
+    );
+
+    // Assert oracle health
+    assert!(
+        oracles_passed,
+        "[{test_name}] Oracle failure at seed {seed}: {:?}",
+        report.oracle_report
+    );
+    assert!(
+        !invariant_violations_found,
+        "[{test_name}] Invariant violations at seed {seed}: {:?}",
+        report.invariant_violations
+    );
+
+    lab_report
+}
+
+/// Convenience: run a lab test with just a seed and closure.
+///
+/// Uses default configuration (2 workers, 100k max steps).
+pub fn run_lab_test_simple<F>(seed: u64, test_name: &str, test_fn: F) -> LabTestReport
+where
+    F: FnOnce(&mut LabRuntime),
+{
+    run_lab_test(LabTestConfig::new(seed, test_name), test_fn)
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// run_chaos_test — fault injection testing
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Configuration for a chaos test run.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
+pub struct ChaosTestConfig {
+    /// Base deterministic test config.
+    pub base: LabTestConfig,
+    /// Chaos injection preset or custom config.
+    pub chaos: ChaosPreset,
+}
+
+/// Chaos injection preset.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
+pub enum ChaosPreset {
+    /// Low-probability faults (suitable for CI).
+    Light,
+    /// High-probability faults (thorough testing).
+    Heavy,
+    /// Custom chaos configuration.
+    Custom(ChaosConfig),
+}
+
+impl ChaosTestConfig {
+    /// Create a chaos test config with light fault injection.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn light(seed: u64, test_name: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            base: LabTestConfig::new(seed, test_name),
+            chaos: ChaosPreset::Light,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Create a chaos test config with heavy fault injection.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn heavy(seed: u64, test_name: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            base: LabTestConfig::new(seed, test_name),
+            chaos: ChaosPreset::Heavy,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Create with a custom ChaosConfig.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn custom(seed: u64, test_name: impl Into<String>, chaos: ChaosConfig) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            base: LabTestConfig::new(seed, test_name),
+            chaos: ChaosPreset::Custom(chaos),
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Set worker count.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn worker_count(mut self, count: usize) -> Self {
+        self.base.worker_count = count;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Set max steps.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn max_steps(mut self, steps: u64) -> Self {
+        self.base.max_steps = steps;
+        self
+    }
+}
+
+/// Report from a chaos test run.
+#[derive(Debug)]
+pub struct ChaosTestReport {
+    /// Base test report.
+    pub base: LabTestReport,
+    /// Whether chaos injection was active.
+    pub chaos_active: bool,
+}
+
+impl ChaosTestReport {
+    /// Returns true if the test passed despite fault injection.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn passed(&self) -> bool {
+        self.base.passed()
+    }
+}
+
+/// Run a chaos test with fault injection.
+///
+/// Same as `run_lab_test` but with chaos injection enabled. The test
+/// closure should be resilient to injected faults (cancellations, delays,
+/// I/O errors).
+///
+/// # Panics
+///
+/// Panics if oracles fail or invariant violations are detected.
+pub fn run_chaos_test<F>(config: ChaosTestConfig, test_fn: F) -> ChaosTestReport
+where
+    F: FnOnce(&mut LabRuntime),
+{
+    let seed = config.base.seed;
+    let test_name = config.base.test_name.clone();
+
+    let mut lab_config = config.base.to_lab_config();
+    lab_config = match config.chaos {
+        ChaosPreset::Light => lab_config.with_light_chaos(),
+        ChaosPreset::Heavy => lab_config.with_heavy_chaos(),
+        ChaosPreset::Custom(c) => lab_config.with_chaos(c),
+    };
+
+    tracing::info!(
+        seed,
+        test_name = %test_name,
+        chaos = true,
+        "Starting chaos LabRuntime test"
+    );
+
+    let mut runtime = LabRuntime::new(lab_config);
+    let chaos_active = runtime.has_chaos();
+
+    test_fn(&mut runtime);
+
+    let report = runtime.run_until_quiescent_with_report();
+    let oracles_passed = report.oracle_report.all_passed();
+    let invariant_violations_found = !report.invariant_violations.is_empty();
+
+    let chaos_stats = runtime.chaos_stats();
+    tracing::info!(
+        seed,
+        test_name = %test_name,
+        steps = runtime.steps(),
+        chaos_active,
+        chaos_decision_points = chaos_stats.decision_points,
+        chaos_delays = chaos_stats.delays,
+        oracles_passed,
+        violations = report.invariant_violations.len(),
+        "Chaos LabRuntime test completed"
+    );
+
+    let base_report = LabTestReport {
+        seed,
+        test_name: test_name.clone(),
+        steps: runtime.steps(),
+        final_time: runtime.now(),
+        oracles_passed,
+        invariant_violations_found,
+    };
+
+    assert!(
+        oracles_passed,
+        "[{test_name}] Chaos oracle failure at seed {seed}: {:?}",
+        report.oracle_report
+    );
+    assert!(
+        !invariant_violations_found,
+        "[{test_name}] Chaos invariant violations at seed {seed}: {:?}",
+        report.invariant_violations
+    );
+
+    ChaosTestReport {
+        base: base_report,
+        chaos_active,
+    }
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// run_exploration_test — DPOR schedule exploration
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Configuration for a schedule exploration run.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
+pub struct ExplorationTestConfig {
+    /// Human-readable test name.
+    pub test_name: String,
+    /// Base seed for exploration (seeds sweep from base_seed..base_seed+max_runs).
+    pub base_seed: u64,
+    /// Maximum number of exploration runs.
+    pub max_runs: usize,
+    /// Maximum steps per individual run.
+    pub max_steps_per_run: u64,
+    /// Number of simulated workers per run.
+    pub worker_count: usize,
+}
+
+impl ExplorationTestConfig {
+    /// Create an exploration config.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn new(test_name: impl Into<String>, max_runs: usize) -> Self {
+        Self {
+            test_name: test_name.into(),
+            base_seed: 0,
+            max_runs,
+            max_steps_per_run: 100_000,
+            worker_count: 2,
+        }
+    }
+
+    /// Set the base seed.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn base_seed(mut self, seed: u64) -> Self {
+        self.base_seed = seed;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Set the worker count.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn worker_count(mut self, count: usize) -> Self {
+        self.worker_count = count;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Set max steps per run.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn max_steps_per_run(mut self, steps: u64) -> Self {
+        self.max_steps_per_run = steps;
+        self
+    }
+
+    /// Convert to asupersync `ExplorerConfig`.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn to_explorer_config(&self) -> ExplorerConfig {
+        ExplorerConfig {
+            base_seed: self.base_seed,
+            max_runs: self.max_runs,
+            max_steps_per_run: self.max_steps_per_run,
+            worker_count: self.worker_count,
+            record_traces: true,
+        }
+    }
+}
+
+/// Report from a schedule exploration.
+#[derive(Debug)]
+pub struct ExplorationTestReport {
+    /// Test name.
+    pub test_name: String,
+    /// Total runs executed.
+    pub total_runs: usize,
+    /// Number of unique equivalence classes discovered.
+    pub unique_classes: usize,
+    /// Whether any violations were found.
+    pub has_violations: bool,
+    /// Seeds that produced violations (for reproduction).
+    pub violation_seeds: Vec<u64>,
+    /// The underlying exploration report.
+    pub inner: ExplorationReport,
+}
+
+impl ExplorationTestReport {
+    /// Returns true if no violations were found.
+    #[must_use]
+    pub fn passed(&self) -> bool {
+        !self.has_violations
+    }
+}
+
+/// Run schedule exploration (DPOR seed-sweep) across multiple seeds.
+///
+/// The `test_fn` receives a mutable reference to a `LabRuntime` for each
+/// seed. It should set up concurrent tasks and drive execution. The explorer
+/// automatically varies the scheduling seed to cover different interleavings.
+///
+/// # Panics
+///
+/// Panics if any violation is found during exploration.
+///
+/// # Example
+///
+/// ```ignore
+/// use crate::common::lab::{ExplorationTestConfig, run_exploration_test};
+///
+/// run_exploration_test(
+///     ExplorationTestConfig::new("concurrent_access", 50),
+///     |runtime| {
+///         // Set up concurrent tasks...
+///         runtime.run_until_quiescent();
+///     },
+/// );
+/// ```
+pub fn run_exploration_test<F>(
+    config: ExplorationTestConfig,
+    test_fn: F,
+) -> ExplorationTestReport
+where
+    F: Fn(&mut LabRuntime),
+{
+    let test_name = config.test_name.clone();
+
+    tracing::info!(
+        test_name = %test_name,
+        base_seed = config.base_seed,
+        max_runs = config.max_runs,
+        workers = config.worker_count,
+        "Starting schedule exploration"
+    );
+
+    let explorer_config = config.to_explorer_config();
+    let mut explorer = ScheduleExplorer::new(explorer_config);
+    let inner = explorer.explore(test_fn);
+
+    let has_violations = inner.has_violations();
+    let violation_seeds: Vec<u64> = inner.violation_seeds().to_vec();
+
+    tracing::info!(
+        test_name = %test_name,
+        total_runs = inner.total_runs,
+        unique_classes = inner.unique_classes,
+        has_violations,
+        violation_count = violation_seeds.len(),
+        "Schedule exploration completed"
+    );
+
+    let report = ExplorationTestReport {
+        test_name: test_name.clone(),
+        total_runs: inner.total_runs,
+        unique_classes: inner.unique_classes,
+        has_violations,
+        violation_seeds: violation_seeds.clone(),
+        inner,
+    };
+
+    assert!(
+        !has_violations,
+        "[{test_name}] Schedule exploration found violations at seeds: {violation_seeds:?}"
+    );
+
+    report
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Convenience: multi-seed lab test (run same test across N seeds)
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Run the same test across multiple seeds, asserting all pass.
+///
+/// Useful for catching seed-dependent bugs without full DPOR exploration.
+pub fn run_multi_seed_test<F>(
+    test_name: &str,
+    seeds: &[u64],
+    test_fn: F,
+) -> Vec<LabTestReport>
+where
+    F: Fn(&mut LabRuntime),
+{
+    let mut reports = Vec::with_capacity(seeds.len());
+    for &seed in seeds {
+        let name = format!("{test_name}/seed-{seed}");
+        let config = LabTestConfig::new(seed, name);
+        let mut runtime = LabRuntime::new(config.to_lab_config());
+        test_fn(&mut runtime);
+
+        let report = runtime.run_until_quiescent_with_report();
+        let oracles_passed = report.oracle_report.all_passed();
+        let invariant_violations_found = !report.invariant_violations.is_empty();
+
+        assert!(
+            oracles_passed,
+            "[{test_name}] Oracle failure at seed {seed}"
+        );
+        assert!(
+            !invariant_violations_found,
+            "[{test_name}] Invariant violations at seed {seed}"
+        );
+
+        reports.push(LabTestReport {
+            seed,
+            test_name: format!("{test_name}/seed-{seed}"),
+            steps: runtime.steps(),
+            final_time: runtime.now(),
+            oracles_passed,
+            invariant_violations_found,
+        });
+    }
+    reports
+}
